schools.summary: The Levite marriage of Moses' parents: 'and there went a man of the house of Levi, and took to wife a daughter of Levi' — a man of the tribe of Levi (Amram, Exodus 6:20) taking to wife a daughter of Levi (Jochebed, the parents of Moses, and Aaron and Miriam, the Levite house, the priestly tribe, the godly parents, commended for their faith, Hebrews 11:23); the Levite descent (Amram and Jochebed, of the house of Kohath, Exodus 6:18-20) — the priestly tribe (the parents of the deliverer, the godly stock); the marriage (Amram and Jochebed) — the providence preparing the birth of Moses (the deliverer, the godly parents, who would hide the child by faith, v.2; Hebrews 11:23, the line of Levi from which the lawgiver and the priesthood would come, Exodus 6:20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: And there went a man of the house of Levi, and took to wife a daughter of Levi
the Levite marriage of Moses' parents
Amram and Jochebed, of the priestly tribe (Exodus 6:20)
the godly parents preparing the birth of the deliverer (Hebrews 11:23)

schools.summary: The birth of Moses and his hiding by faith: 'and the woman conceived, and bare a son: and when she saw him that he was a goodly child, she hid him three months' — Jochebed bearing a son (Moses), seeing he was a 'goodly' (fair, beautiful) child — the 'exceeding fair' (Acts 7:20, 'fair to God'), perhaps marking him as one set apart by God, hiding him three months (defying Pharaoh's edict, Exodus 1:22, the hiding of the child); the 'goodly' child (Acts 7:20, 'exceeding fair'; Hebrews 11:23, 'a proper child') — the special beauty (perhaps a token of God's favor, moving the parents' faith, the child marked out); 'she hid him three months' (the parents, by faith, hiding the child, defying the king's edict, Exodus 1:22, not fearing the king's wrath, the faith specially commended — 'By faith Moses, when he was born, was hid three months of his parents, because they saw he was a proper child; and they were not afraid of the king's commandment,' Hebrews 11:23, the courage of faith, the fear of God above the fear of the king, as the midwives, Exodus 1:17, the providence preserving the deliverer) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: And the woman conceived, and bare a son: and when she saw him that he was a goodly child, she hid him three months
the birth of Moses and his hiding by faith
the 'goodly child,' exceeding fair, marked out by God (Acts 7:20; Hebrews 11:23)
the parents by faith defying the king's edict (Hebrews 11:23; Exodus 1:17, 22)

schools.summary: Moses placed in the ark of bulrushes: 'and when she could not longer hide him, she took for him an ark of bulrushes, and daubed it with slime and with pitch, and put the child therein; and she laid it in the flags by the river's brink' — Jochebed, unable to hide Moses longer (the child growing, the danger increasing), making a little ark (a basket, a chest) of bulrushes (papyrus), waterproofed with slime (bitumen) and pitch, and placing the child in it (the providence, the ark, like Noah's, a vessel of preservation through the water, Genesis 6:14, the same word 'pitch,' the faith committing the child to God); the 'ark' (the basket of papyrus, the word echoing Noah's ark, Genesis 6:14) — the little vessel of preservation (the faith that, having done what it could, committed the child to God's care); 'she laid it in the flags by the river's brink' (the ark laid among the reeds at the river's edge, where Pharaoh's daughter would come, v.5, the faith, committing the child to the very river of death, Exodus 1:22, trusting God to preserve him, the providence, the place chosen, by God's ordering, where the child would be found and saved, vv.5-10) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And when she could not longer hide him, she took for him an ark of bulrushes... and she laid it in the flags by the river's brink
Moses placed in the ark of bulrushes
the little vessel of preservation, like Noah's ark through the water (Genesis 6:14)
the faith committing the child to God at the very river of death (Exodus 1:22; vv.5-10)

schools.summary: Miriam watching over the ark: 'and his sister stood afar off, to wit what would be done to him' — Moses' sister (Miriam, Exodus 15:20; Numbers 26:59) standing at a distance to watch what would happen to the child (the loving care of the sister, the watching, that she might act, as she did, v.7-8, to bring the mother as nurse, the providence using the sister's vigilance); Miriam's watching (from a distance, to see the child's fate) — the loving vigilance (the sister set to watch, the care of the family); 'to wit what would be done to him' (Miriam watching to know the child's fate, and ready to act, the providence, the sister's watching that would bring the mother to nurse the child, vv.7-9, the care that God would use to preserve and restore the child) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And his sister stood afar off, to wit what would be done to him
Miriam watching over the ark
the loving vigilance of the sister (Numbers 26:59)
the care God would use to bring the mother to nurse the child (vv.7-9)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's daughter finding the ark: 'and the daughter of Pharaoh came down to wash herself at the river; and her maidens walked along by the river's side; and when she saw the ark among the flags, she sent her maid to fetch it' — Pharaoh's daughter coming to the river to bathe, seeing the ark among the reeds, and sending her maid to fetch it (the providence, the very daughter of the king who decreed the death, by God's ordering, coming to the spot, finding and saving the child, the divine direction of events, the child found by the one who could protect him); the finding of the ark (by Pharaoh's daughter, the very court that sought the child's death) — the providence (the child found by the one who would save him, the divine overruling of the edict); 'she sent her maid to fetch it' (the ark fetched, the child about to be found, v.6, the providence, the deliverer drawn from the river of death, raised in the very palace of the oppressor, the irony, Moses nursed by his own mother, v.8-9, and reared as Pharaoh's daughter's son, v.10, at Pharaoh's own cost, God overruling the plot to preserve and prepare the deliverer) — Henry, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the daughter of Pharaoh came down to wash herself at the river... when she saw the ark among the flags, she sent her maid to fetch it
Pharaoh's daughter finding the ark
the providence - the child saved by the very court that sought his death
the deliverer drawn from the river, to be reared in the oppressor's palace (vv.6-10)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's daughter's compassion on the weeping babe: 'and when she had opened it, she saw the child: and, behold, the babe wept. And she had compassion on him, and said, This is one of the Hebrews' children' — Pharaoh's daughter opening the ark and seeing the child, and the babe weeping (the tears of the infant, moving her pity, the discovery of the Hebrew child, the providence, the babe's weeping stirring the princess's compassion); the princess's compassion (her pity moved by the weeping babe) — the providence using a woman's tenderness to save the child (the compassion overruling the edict, the king's daughter pitying the child the king would drown, the mercy that preserved the deliverer); 'This is one of the Hebrews' children' (the princess recognizing the child as a Hebrew, under the edict, Exodus 1:22, yet, in compassion, saving him, the providence, the child known to be a Hebrew, yet spared and reared in the palace, the deliverer preserved by the oppressor's daughter) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And when she had opened it, she saw the child: and, behold, the babe wept. And she had compassion on him, and said, This is one of the Hebrews' children
Pharaoh's daughter's compassion on the weeping babe
the babe's weeping stirring the princess's pity
the king's daughter pitying the child the king would drown (Exodus 1:22)

schools.summary: Miriam offering to fetch a Hebrew nurse: 'then said his sister to Pharaoh's daughter, Shall I go and call to thee a nurse of the Hebrew women, that she may nurse the child for thee?' — Miriam (watching, v.4), seeing the princess's compassion, stepping forward and offering to fetch a Hebrew nurse for the child (the ready wit of the sister, the providence, the offer that would bring the child's own mother to nurse him, vv.8-9, the loving care); Miriam's offer (to fetch a Hebrew nurse) — the providence (the very plan to restore the child to his mother, the wit and care of the sister, the divine ordering); Miriam's offer — the means (by God's providence) of restoring Moses to his own mother (to be nursed, vv.8-9, and reared, at Pharaoh's cost, the care that God used, the deliverer given back to his godly home for his early years) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Then said his sister to Pharaoh's daughter, Shall I go and call to thee a nurse of the Hebrew women, that she may nurse the child for thee?
Miriam offering to fetch a Hebrew nurse
the ready wit of the watching sister (v.4)
the providence restoring the child to his own mother (vv.8-9)

schools.summary: The child's own mother called as nurse: 'and Pharaoh's daughter said to her, Go. And the maid went and called the child's mother' — the princess agreeing, and Miriam going and calling the child's own mother (Jochebed, the providence, the mother brought to nurse her own child, by the princess's leave, at the palace's cost, the restoration of the child to his mother, the divine ordering); the princess's leave (sending Miriam to fetch the nurse) — the providence (the way opened for the mother); 'the maid went and called the child's mother' (Jochebed called to nurse her own son, the providence restoring the child to his mother, the irony, the mother paid to nurse her own child, vv.9, by the oppressor's daughter, God overruling all to preserve and prepare the deliverer in his own godly home) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Pharaoh's daughter said to her, Go. And the maid went and called the child's mother
the child's own mother called as nurse
the providence restoring the child to his mother (Jochebed)
the irony - the mother paid to nurse her own son by the oppressor's daughter (v.9)

schools.summary: Jochebed paid to nurse her own son: 'and Pharaoh's daughter said unto her, Take this child away, and nurse it for me, and I will give thee thy wages. And the woman took the child, and nursed it' — the princess charging Jochebed to nurse the child for her, promising wages, Jochebed taking her own son and nursing him (the providence, the mother given back her child, and paid to raise him, by the very court that sought his death, the deliverer reared in his own godly home for his early, formative years); the wages (Jochebed paid to nurse her own child) — the irony and the providence (God restoring the child and providing for him through the oppressor, the divine overruling); 'the woman took the child, and nursed it' (Jochebed nursing her own son, the early years in the godly home, where she would teach him his people and his God, the foundation of the faith by which Moses would later refuse Egypt, Hebrews 11:24-26, the providence preparing the deliverer) — Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And Pharaoh's daughter said unto her, Take this child away, and nurse it for me, and I will give thee thy wages. And the woman took the child, and nursed it
Jochebed paid to nurse her own son
the providence restoring the child and providing through the oppressor
the deliverer reared in the godly home, the foundation of his later faith (Hebrews 11:24-26)

schools.summary: Moses adopted by Pharaoh's daughter and named: 'and the child grew, and she brought him unto Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son. And she called his name Moses: and she said, Because I drew him out of the water' — Moses, weaned, brought to Pharaoh's daughter and adopted as her son (the providence, the Hebrew child reared in the Egyptian palace, 'learned in all the wisdom of the Egyptians,' Acts 7:22, prepared for his future task, the deliverer in the court of the oppressor); Moses adopted (the son of Pharaoh's daughter) — the providence (the deliverer reared and educated in the palace, yet keeping the faith of his mother, Hebrews 11:24, the strange ordering, the oppressor's house nurturing Israel's deliverer); 'she called his name Moses... Because I drew him out of the water' (the name Moses, Egyptian 'mesu,' son/born, or Hebrew, 'drawn out,' given because he was drawn out of the water, the name a memorial of his deliverance, and a type, the fathers note, of him who would draw Israel out of the waters, and of Christ, drawn up from death, the deliverer named for his being drawn from the water) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the child grew, and she brought him unto Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son. And she called his name Moses... Because I drew him out of the water
Moses adopted by Pharaoh's daughter and named
the Hebrew child reared in the palace, learned in Egypt's wisdom (Acts 7:22), yet keeping the faith (Hebrews 11:24)
the name Moses ('drawn out'), a memorial of his deliverance

schools.summary: Moses, grown, going out to his brethren: 'and it came to pass in those days, when Moses was grown, that he went out unto his brethren, and looked on their burdens: and he spied an Egyptian smiting an Hebrew, one of his brethren' — Moses, now grown (forty years old, Acts 7:23), 'went out unto his brethren, and looked on their burdens' — Moses, though reared as Pharaoh's daughter's son in all the wealth and honor of Egypt, going out to his enslaved brethren the Hebrews and looking on (with sympathy, identifying himself with) their burdens (the great choice — Moses, by faith, owning his afflicted people and his God rather than the honors of Egypt, the faith specially commended: 'By faith Moses, when he was come to years, refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter; choosing rather to suffer affliction with the people of God, than to enjoy the pleasures of sin for a season; esteeming the reproach of Christ greater riches than the treasures in Egypt,' Hebrews 11:24-26, the deliberate, faith-filled choice of the suffering people of God over the world); Moses seeing an Egyptian beating a Hebrew (one of his brethren) — the occasion of his action (v.12, the sympathy with his oppressed people, the indignation at the wrong, the first stirring of the deliverer's zeal); Moses' going out to his brethren and owning their burdens — the proof of his faith (identifying with the despised, enslaved people of God, esteeming their reproach above Egypt's treasures, Hebrews 11:26, the choice that prepared the deliverer, the faith that, though Moses' zeal then outran God's time, vv.12-15, was the right and commended choice of God's people over the world) — Calvin, Wesley, Henry, Gill.

debates.question: What does Moses' going out to his brethren display?

sides.position: Moses, reared in Pharaoh's palace, by faith owning his afflicted brethren and his God rather than the honors of Egypt - the great choice commended in Hebrews 11:24-26 (refusing to be called Pharaoh's daughter's son, choosing affliction with God's people, esteeming the reproach of Christ above Egypt's treasures)

themes: And it came to pass in those days, when Moses was grown, that he went out unto his brethren, and looked on their burdens: and he spied an Egyptian smiting an Hebrew
Moses, grown, going out to his brethren (the crux of his faith and choice)
Moses by faith owning his afflicted people and his God rather than the honors of Egypt (Acts 7:23)
the great choice commended in Hebrews 11:24-26 - affliction with God's people above Egypt's treasures

schools.summary: Moses slaying the Egyptian: 'and he looked this way and that way, and when he saw that there was no man, he slew the Egyptian, and hid him in the sand' — Moses, seeing the Egyptian smiting the Hebrew (v.11), looking about, and finding none watching, killing the Egyptian and hiding the body in the sand (the deed, defending his brother, the deliverer's zeal, smiting the oppressor, the act done secretly); Moses' act variously regarded: (a) by faith, as a deliverer beginning his work (Acts 7:24-25, 'he supposed his brethren would have understood how that God by his hand would deliver them,' Moses acting as the appointed deliverer, avenging his oppressed brother, his zeal commended in substance though premature); (b) yet the manner (the private killing, the haste, before God's time and call) generally held a fault — Moses' zeal outrunning God's time and method (the right cause, but the wrong way, taking the matter into his own hand, leading to his flight, v.15, and the forty years' delay in Midian, the witnesses balancing: Moses' zeal for his people was right and God-given, but the rash, private deed was premature, and God would call him in his own time, ch. 3, to deliver by God's power, not man's violence); 'hid him in the sand' (Moses hiding the body, the deed secret, yet to be known, v.14, the providence, the deed that drove Moses to Midian, where God would meet and call him, ch. 3, the deliverer's premature zeal overruled to prepare him in the wilderness) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: How is Moses' slaying of the Egyptian to be judged?

sides.position: His zeal for his oppressed brethren was right and God-given, acting as the appointed deliverer (Acts 7:24-25, his brethren should have understood God would deliver by his hand)

Yet the rash, private killing before God's time and call was premature and faulty - the right cause in the wrong way, leading to his flight and the forty years' preparation in Midian; God would deliver in his own time by his own power

themes: And he looked this way and that way, and when he saw that there was no man, he slew the Egyptian, and hid him in the sand
Moses slaying the Egyptian
his zeal for his brethren right and God-given, acting as the appointed deliverer (Acts 7:24-25)
yet the rash, private deed before God's time premature, leading to Midian

schools.summary: Moses rebuking the Hebrew wrongdoer: 'and when he went out the second day, behold, two men of the Hebrews strove together: and he said to him that did the wrong, Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?' — Moses, going out again, finding two Hebrews fighting, and rebuking the one in the wrong (the aggressor) — 'why smitest thou thy fellow?' (the effort to reconcile his brethren, the deliverer seeking to be a peacemaker, the rebuke of the wrongdoer); Moses' rebuke (to the one in the wrong) — the attempt to make peace among his brethren (the deliverer's concern for justice among his own, the rebuke that would be rejected, v.14); 'Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?' (Moses' appeal, why wrong thy brother, thy fellow Hebrew?, the peacemaking effort, the rejection to come, v.14, the wrongdoer turning on Moses, the deliverer rejected by his own, as Christ, John 1:11; Acts 7:25-27) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And when he went out the second day, behold, two men of the Hebrews strove together: and he said to him that did the wrong, Wherefore smitest thou thy fellow?
Moses rebuking the Hebrew wrongdoer
the deliverer seeking to make peace among his brethren
soon rejected by his own (as Christ, John 1:11; Acts 7:25-27)

schools.summary: The wrongdoer rejecting Moses, and Moses' fear: 'and he said, Who made thee a prince and a judge over us? intendest thou to kill me, as thou killedst the Egyptian? And Moses feared, and said, Surely this thing is known' — the wrongdoer turning on Moses, rejecting his authority ('who made thee a prince and a judge?') and revealing that the killing of the Egyptian (v.12) was known (the rejection of the deliverer by his own, Acts 7:27-28, 'But he that did his neighbour wrong thrust him away,' the ingratitude and unbelief of the brethren); the rejection — the brethren refusing Moses as ruler and judge (Acts 7:35, 'This Moses whom they refused... the same did God send to be a ruler and a deliverer') — a striking type of Christ, rejected by his own (John 1:11, the very words echoed of Christ, 'who made thee a ruler?', Luke 12:14, the deliverer spurned by the people he came to save); 'Moses feared, and said, Surely this thing is known' (Moses, finding the deed known, and so his life in danger from Pharaoh, v.15, fearing, the fear that drove him to flee, v.15, the providence, the rejection and flight that sent Moses to Midian, to be prepared by God for forty years, ch. 3) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What does the rejection of Moses foreshadow?

sides.position: A striking type of Christ rejected by his own (John 1:11) - the deliverer the brethren refused, the same God sent to be ruler and deliverer (Acts 7:35); the words echoed of Christ, 'who made thee a ruler?' (Luke 12:14)

themes: And he said, Who made thee a prince and a judge over us?... And Moses feared, and said, Surely this thing is known
the wrongdoer rejecting Moses, and Moses' fear
the deliverer rejected by his own (Acts 7:27-28, 35), a type of Christ (John 1:11)
Moses' fear at the deed being known, driving him to flee (v.15)

schools.summary: Moses fleeing to Midian: 'now when Pharaoh heard this thing, he sought to slay Moses. But Moses fled from the face of Pharaoh, and dwelt in the land of Midian: and he sat down by a well' — Pharaoh, hearing of the killing (v.12) and Moses' siding with the Hebrews, seeking to kill him, Moses fleeing to Midian (in the Sinai peninsula or Arabia, the flight, by faith, Hebrews 11:27, not fearing the king, or, the danger past, removing himself, the deliverer in exile); Moses' flight to Midian (forsaking Egypt, Hebrews 11:27, 'By faith he forsook Egypt, not fearing the wrath of the king') — the long exile (forty years, Acts 7:30, the providence, the wilderness years preparing Moses, humbling the man of Egypt's court into the shepherd, fitting him for his task, ch. 3, the deliverer in the school of the desert); 'he sat down by a well' (Moses resting by a well in Midian, the place of his meeting with Reuel's daughters, vv.16-21, the providence, the well where God would provide him a home and wife, the exile that prepared the deliverer) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Now when Pharaoh heard this thing, he sought to slay Moses. But Moses fled from the face of Pharaoh, and dwelt in the land of Midian: and he sat down by a well
Moses fleeing to Midian
the flight forsaking Egypt by faith (Hebrews 11:27)
the forty years' exile preparing the deliverer in the desert (Acts 7:30)

schools.summary: The seven daughters of the priest of Midian at the well: 'now the priest of Midian had seven daughters: and they came and drew water, and filled the troughs to water their father's flock' — the priest (or prince) of Midian (Reuel/Jethro, vv.18; Exodus 3:1) having seven daughters who came to draw water for their father's flock (the pastoral scene, the daughters as shepherdesses, the providence, the meeting that would give Moses a home and wife, vv.17-21); the priest of Midian (Reuel, or Jethro, a priest or prince, perhaps of the true God, a descendant of Abraham through Keturah, Genesis 25:2) — Moses' future father-in-law (the godly, or partly godly, household); 'they came and drew water, and filled the troughs to water their father's flock' (the daughters watering the flock, the pastoral life, the well-scene, like Rebekah, Genesis 24; Rachel, Genesis 29, the providence at the well, the meeting that would provide Moses a home, vv.17-21) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: Now the priest of Midian had seven daughters: and they came and drew water, and filled the troughs to water their father's flock
the seven daughters of the priest of Midian at the well
Reuel/Jethro, the priest of Midian, Moses' future father-in-law (Genesis 25:2)
the well-scene where Moses would find his home (as Genesis 24; 29)

schools.summary: Moses defending the daughters and watering their flock: 'and the shepherds came and drove them away: but Moses stood up and helped them, and watered their flock' — when shepherds came and drove the daughters from the well, Moses standing up, defending them, and watering their flock (the chivalry of Moses, defending the weak against the strong, the same zeal for justice, as with the Egyptian and the Hebrews, vv.11-13, now rightly used, the helpfulness of the exile); Moses' help (defending the daughters, watering the flock) — the kindness and courage (the standing for the wronged, the character of the deliverer, a helper of the oppressed); 'watered their flock' (Moses watering the flock, the kind service, the providence, the help that would lead to Reuel's hospitality and Moses' marriage, vv.18-21, the exile finding a home through his kindness) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the shepherds came and drove them away: but Moses stood up and helped them, and watered their flock
Moses defending the daughters and watering their flock
the chivalry that defended the weak against the strong
the kindness that would find the exile a home (vv.18-21)

schools.summary: The daughters returning early to Reuel: 'and when they came to Reuel their father, he said, How is it that ye are come so soon to day?' — the daughters returning to their father Reuel (Jethro, Exodus 3:1) sooner than usual (Moses' help having sped their watering, Reuel's question, surprised at their early return, the occasion of the inquiry, vv.19-20, that would bring Moses to the household); Reuel (also called Jethro, Exodus 3:1, and Raguel, Numbers 10:29, the priest/prince of Midian) — the father of the seven daughters (Moses' future father-in-law); 'How is it that ye are come so soon to day?' (Reuel's surprise, the early return, owing to Moses' help, v.17, the question that drew out the account, vv.19, and led to Moses' welcome, vv.20-21, the providence bringing Moses to the home) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: And when they came to Reuel their father, he said, How is it that ye are come so soon to day?
the daughters returning early to Reuel
Reuel/Jethro, the priest of Midian (Exodus 3:1)
the early return drawing out the account that brought Moses to the household (vv.19-20)

schools.summary: The daughters reporting Moses' help: 'and they said, An Egyptian delivered us out of the hand of the shepherds, and also drew water enough for us, and watered the flock' — the daughters reporting how an 'Egyptian' (Moses, taken for an Egyptian by his dress and speech) had delivered them from the shepherds and watered their flock (the account of Moses' help, v.17, the occasion of Reuel's hospitality, vv.20-21); Moses taken for an Egyptian (by his Egyptian dress, manner, or speech, reared in the palace) — the daughters not knowing he was a Hebrew (the report of the stranger's kindness); 'drew water enough for us, and watered the flock' (Moses' generous help, drawing water, watering the flock, the kindness reported, the occasion, Reuel's desire to reward and welcome him, vv.20-21) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And they said, An Egyptian delivered us out of the hand of the shepherds, and also drew water enough for us, and watered the flock
the daughters reporting Moses' help
Moses taken for an Egyptian by his dress and manner
the kindness that drew Reuel's hospitality (vv.20-21)

schools.summary: Reuel calling Moses to hospitality: 'and he said unto his daughters, And where is he? why is it that ye have left the man? call him, that he may eat bread' — Reuel, hearing of Moses' kindness (v.19), rebuking his daughters for leaving the stranger unthanked and bidding them call him to eat bread (to a meal, the token of hospitality and welcome, the hospitality of Reuel, the welcome that would give Moses a home, v.21); Reuel's concern (that the kind stranger be welcomed, not left) — the duty of hospitality (Hebrews 13:2, the gracious welcome); 'call him, that he may eat bread' (Reuel's invitation, to a meal, the beginning of Moses' dwelling with him, v.21, the providence, the hospitality that gave Moses a home, a wife, and forty years' preparation in Midian, the welcome of the exile) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And he said unto his daughters, And where is he? why is it that ye have left the man? call him, that he may eat bread
Reuel calling Moses to hospitality
the gracious welcome of the kind stranger (Hebrews 13:2)
the hospitality that gave Moses a home and forty years in Midian (v.21)

schools.summary: Moses dwelling with Reuel and marrying Zipporah: 'and Moses was content to dwell with the man: and he gave Moses Zipporah his daughter' — Moses agreeing to dwell with Reuel (Jethro), and Reuel giving him his daughter Zipporah to wife (the home found in Midian, the marriage, Zipporah, the mother of Gershom and Eliezer, Exodus 18:3-4, the providence settling the exile); Moses' contentment (to dwell in Midian, the exile finding a home) — the providence (the shepherd-life, forty years, Acts 7:30, that would humble and prepare him); 'he gave Moses Zipporah his daughter' (Moses' marriage to Zipporah, the Midianite, daughter of the priest, the providence, the home, the wife, the years of preparation in the desert, fitting Moses, the man of Egypt's court, into the humble shepherd whom God would call, ch. 3) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Moses was content to dwell with the man: and he gave Moses Zipporah his daughter
Moses dwelling with Reuel and marrying Zipporah
the home and wife found in Midian (Exodus 18:3-4)
the years preparing the man of Egypt's court into the humble shepherd (Acts 7:30)

schools.summary: The birth and naming of Gershom: 'and she bare him a son, and he called his name Gershom: for he said, I have been a stranger in a strange land' — Zipporah bearing Moses a son, named Gershom ('a stranger there,' or 'exile,' the name commemorating Moses' condition as a stranger and exile in Midian, the testimony of his pilgrim state); Moses' word — 'a stranger in a strange land': Moses owning himself an exile and pilgrim (in Midian, far from his people, the pilgrim spirit of the godly, strangers on earth, looking for the homeland, Hebrews 11:13-16, the faith that, even in exile, held to the covenant hope, the stranger far from the promised land); Gershom (the name of exile) — Moses' testimony to his pilgrim state (a stranger in Midian, his heart still with his people and the promise, the faith that did not forget Israel and the covenant, the deliverer waiting, in exile, for God's time, vv.23-25, the pilgrim hope) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And she bare him a son, and he called his name Gershom: for he said, I have been a stranger in a strange land
the birth and naming of Gershom
the name commemorating Moses' exile and pilgrim state
the pilgrim spirit of the godly, strangers looking for the homeland (Hebrews 11:13-16)

schools.summary: Israel's cry rising to God under the bondage: 'and it came to pass in process of time, that the king of Egypt died: and the children of Israel sighed by reason of the bondage, and they cried, and their cry came up unto God by reason of the bondage' — after many years (Moses' forty in Midian, Acts 7:30), the king of Egypt dying (yet the bondage continuing, or growing worse), Israel, groaning under the bondage, crying out (the long affliction, the cry of the oppressed); Israel's sighing and crying (under the cruel bondage) — the groaning of the oppressed (the cry wrung out by affliction, whether a cry to God in prayer, or the cry of misery that God heard, the affliction ripe for deliverance); 'their cry came up unto God' (the cry rising to God, who hears the cry of the afflicted, Psalm 12:5, the groaning that moved the providence toward deliverance, the turning-point, the affliction ripe, the time of the promise drawing near, Genesis 15:13-16, God about to act, vv.24-25) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And it came to pass in process of time, that the king of Egypt died: and the children of Israel sighed by reason of the bondage, and they cried, and their cry came up unto God by reason of the bondage
Israel's cry rising to God under the bondage
the groaning of the oppressed rising to God who hears (Psalm 12:5)
the affliction ripe, the time of the promise drawing near (Genesis 15:13-16)

schools.summary: God hearing and remembering his covenant: 'and God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob' — God hearing Israel's groaning under the bondage (v.23, the God who hears the cry of the afflicted, Psalm 34:17, the turning of God toward his people, the answer to the long cry); 'God remembered his covenant' — God 'remembering' his covenant with the patriarchs (Genesis 15:13-18; 17:7-8; 26:3; 28:13-15, not that God had ever forgotten, he cannot forget, but the language of accommodation: God 'remembered' in the sense of now acting on the covenant, bringing to pass the promised deliverance at the appointed time, the four hundred years drawing to their close, Genesis 15:13-16, the covenant the ground of the deliverance, God delivering not for Israel's worth, but for his covenant and oath to the fathers, Deuteronomy 7:8; Psalm 105:8-10, 42, the faithfulness of God to his covenant, the sure ground of all his mercies, the turning-point of the Exodus — God remembering, and about to act, Exodus 3); God's remembering his covenant — the comfort to the afflicted (God's covenant the sure ground of deliverance, the providence, the covenant kept, the deliverance begun, the answer to Joseph's dying faith, Genesis 50:24, 'God will surely visit you,' the faithfulness of God moving to redeem his people) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: In what sense did God 'remember' his covenant?

sides.position: Not that God had ever forgotten (he cannot forget), but the language of accommodation for his now acting on the covenant at the appointed time (Genesis 15:13-18) - delivering Israel for his oath to the fathers (Deuteronomy 7:8; Psalm 105:8-10), the covenant the sure ground of the deliverance, the answer to Joseph's dying faith (Genesis 50:24)

themes: And God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob
God hearing and remembering his covenant (the crux of the covenant-remembrance)
not forgetting, but acting on the covenant at the appointed time (Genesis 15:13-18; Deuteronomy 7:8)
the covenant the sure ground of the deliverance, the answer to Joseph's dying faith (Genesis 50:24; Psalm 105:8-10)

schools.summary: God looking upon Israel with regard: 'and God looked upon the children of Israel, and God had respect unto them' — God looking upon (regarding, taking notice of) the afflicted Israelites (the eye of God turning to his people in mercy, the divine notice of their suffering, the answer to the cry, v.23, the turning toward deliverance); God's looking (with pity and care, as he looks on the afflicted, Psalm 25:18) — the merciful regard (the eye of God on his suffering people, the prelude to action); 'God had respect unto them' (Hebrew, 'God knew,' took knowledge, regarded, acknowledged them) — God acknowledging his people (owning them in their affliction, knowing their sorrows, as Exodus 3:7, 'I know their sorrows,' the four verbs of v.24-25 marking the full turning of God toward the deliverance, God heard, remembered, looked, knew, the providence, God's regard moving to the call of Moses and the Exodus, ch. 3, the covenant God owning his afflicted people) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And God looked upon the children of Israel, and God had respect unto them
God looking upon Israel with regard
the eye of God turning to his afflicted people in mercy (Psalm 25:18)
'God knew' them - the four verbs (heard, remembered, looked, knew) marking the turning toward deliverance (Exodus 3:7)
